content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660              322-5901            322-6441          322-6444


May 20, 1980


Mr. Robert L. Shand
City Clerk-Treasurer
City of Downey
P.O. Box 607
Downey, CA  90241

Dear Mr. Shand:

     By letter of April 25, 1980, you have inquired whether any
reporting obligation has been incurred by Joel H. Lubin by reason
of Mr. Lubin's "Paid Political Announcement" appearing in the
April 6, 1980, Southeast Daily News.

     Because, as we understand the facts, Mr. Lubin was not a
candidate, or an official, controlled or primary purpose committee,
the standard for determining whether he has any reporting obligation
is whether his communication "expressly advocates" an election
result. We do not believe that the material meets that standard
as interpreted by the relevant case law. Therefore, Mr. Lubin
has no reporting obligation pursuant to the Political Reform Act.

     If I can be of further assistance to you, please do not
hesitate to contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Thomas M. Sobel
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

TMS:nrw

City of Downey

April 25, 1980


Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Attention: Regina Siciliano-Kutchins
Technical Assistance Program Coordinator

Subject: Paid Political Announcement

Gentlemen:

Enclosed with this letter is a clipping from the daily Southeast
News under date of Sunday, April 6, 1980.

The City of Downey conducted a general municipal election on
April 8, 1980, wherein various named candidates who have been referred to in the enclosed newspaper clipping ran for election.
The author of the newspaper article, Joel H. Lubin, was not a
candidate in the April 8th election.

To date, no evidence or word of any existing election campaign
committee involving Mr. Lubin or any possible associates has been
brought to my attention.

My question is whether or not the enclosed paid political announcement constitutes an election campaign status, and if should it
involve filing the necessary campaign identification number
application? Although the newspaper publisher did not indicate
the cost of the enclosed publication, it has been estimated that
the expenses exceeded $500. I am aware that the publication
appeared on two separate days.

Very truly yours,

Robert L. Shand
City Clerk-Treasurer

RLS:jm
Encl.

8425 SECOND STREET        P.O. BOX 607        DOWNEY, CALIFORNIA 90241        (213) 861-0361

BY CHAMPION—PARAMOUNT NEWS—

A-3

PAID POLITICAL ANNOUNCEMENT

JOEL H. LUBIN, PE
7820 Allengrove Street
Downey, California 90240
April 4, 1980

Let's set the record straight about the race in District 3 where Bob Cormack is
now getting the headlines over his relationship with Councilman Mackaig and
others who supported Milty in his efforts to Clean-up Downey.
Last year at a Downey Chamber of Commerce Mixer, Milty openly told me the
details of his plan to get control of the Downey City Council and the seat now held by
Assemblyman Bruce Young.
Here is Milty's plan as he told it to me and everyone who was in earshot. He said
that he was going to get Bob Cormack to pretend to run for re-election to the
Downey School Board and then drop out in favor of the City Council race. Cormack
was his choice because Milty figured that Bob would be able to beat Councilman
William "Bill" Greene. Milty also told me that he was backing Jim Santangelo for
District 5 and Ted Jackman for re-election to District 1. Milty was then going to run
for the Republican nomination for Bruce Young's Assembly Seat using his fine base
in Downey to wipe out Bruce. He also had plans to run Mrs. Barbara Hayden for
Bob Cormack's School Board seat and to support School Board members D.
Clayton Mayes and John Shea for re-election. He was looking at that time for a candidate to run for Mr. (Robert) Comstock's seat.
I told Mayor Ken Miller about this at the mixer and phoned Bruce Young to inform him that Milty not Bill Greene would be his 1980 Assembly opponent. I told Bill
Greene about this soon thereafter. Bill told me that he was planning to run for reelection and would welcome a contest against Bob Cormack who would be perceived as this year's version of Neal Cialborne. Bruce Young told me that whoever the
Republicans run will have to beat his record and that he will take whoever wins the
primary seriously.
Now, what has really happened: Bob Cormack took papers out to run for School
Board but failed to return them. Barbara Hayden took out papers and then
withdrew in favor of Randall Barb. Shea and Mayes both ran and were re-elected
with Milty's help. Barbara Hayden is now shown on Cormack's ads as his campaign
treasurer. Cormack's yard signs look like Randall Barb's.
Greene decided to retire and Barrie Dasso decided to run for District 3. My wife
has known her as they are both teachers in Downey and suggested that I let her run
and that I should not run for Bill Greene's seat myself and that Mrs. Dasso has
demonstrated more ability in her professional activities than Hazel Scotto
demonstrated during her term on the council. I met Mrs. Dasso and decided that
my wife was right. I decided to endorse Mrs. Dasso for District 3 and did so before
learning that she is a registered Republican as are Bob Cormack and Mr. Zacker.
For this action I now face censure by both the state Democratic Central Committee
and by the Los Angeles County Central Committee. I am a member of both; ViceChairman of the 63rd AD County Committee and Secretary of the 63rd AD
Democratic Party District Committee.
Ted Jackman has decided to run against Santangelo for District 5 forcing Milty to
make a decision. Milty has said to me that he is supporting Santangelo. Milty has said
that he is supporting Quinn in District 1. Milty has kept his mouth shut for the past
two weeks, but has been seen placing Santangelo signs and Quinn signs in local
businesses.

Republican Central Committee members have told me that they worked hard
and long hours to get Milty out of the Bruce Young race in favor of Mrs. Sally
Brandes fearing that Milty on the ballot in November will cause Senator Bill Camp-
bell to lose to "None of the Above" or my nine-year-old son, and/or his beagle,
Brandt.

Also I find it hard to believe that William Greene is supporting Santangelo for
District 5 as Jim is showing in his ad that was run in the local weekly. Councilman
William "Bill" Greene has openly endorsed Ted Jackman for District 5 to replace
Mayor Ken Miller.

In summary, I believe Bob Cormack in his concern that he is now being stained
by Milty. Bob must realize that guilt by association is common in politics. But, after
a careful review of the facts and my own further investigation I can only believe
that Bob Cormack should retire from office for a while and develop a better collec-
tion of friends. I must believe what Milty told me last year and I cannot nor will not
assume that my long discussion with Milty was a dream or a fantasy as I do not
drink or take pot.

I have reviewed the $210 Milty received with two attorneys. Both are specialists
in municipal law.

Furthermore, I have agreed to chair a re-call attempt to remove Lyell Swear-
ingen from office. This is based on the fact that Milty and Lyell vote together and
operate politically together over 90 percent of the time. While I know that Milty is
the leader, Lyell can only be in full agreement or is senile.

For those of you who don't remember: I was Vice-Chairman and Technical Ad-
visor to the City Staff of the City of Downey Tax Impact Study Committee since early 1977. While we were given credit for removing the Utility User's Tax before
Prop. 13, I believe that credit must go to Mayor Ken Miller for his leadership and to
Councilmember Bill Greene and former Council member Hazel Scotto for joining
Ken in voting to eliminate this awful unfair tax.

For your further information, I asked the Downey City Council to convene a
meeting of the Tax Impact Study Committee the same night that Mayor Miller an-
nounced the charges against Milty. Since Dr. Carolyn De Olden has left Downey, I
am acting Chairman and will continue until Mayor Miller leaves office. At that
time I will recommend that Ken Miller take over the committee and that I continue
as Vice-Chairman until we finish a careful evaluation of Proposition 9 (Jarvis II).
For purposes of debate and my current view of this situation, I feel Prop. 9 and
I believe that Ken Miller opposes it. This is the reverse of the situation regarding
the Utility Users Tax in 1977 when Ken opposed the tax and I supported it at the
start, but I switched when we finally understood the facts that the City Staff compil-
ed with the help of the JayCee's.

I will be out of town for the election on Monday and Tuesday due to official state
business in San Francisco, so therefore I have voted absentee for Barrie Dasso and
Ted Jackman. I will not take a travel advance because I believe that I can afford to
carry the State of California for two weeks while I wait for reimbursement of my
true expenses. Perhaps Milty should do the same with his future travels. The
Downey taxpayers should not be required to support Milty in his bizarre approach
to Cleaning-up Downey. He needs to clean-up his act before he criticizes others like
City Manager Charles Thompson and the rapidly vanishing City Staff.

Sincerely,

Joel H. Lubin
Professional Engineer


PAID POLITICAL ANNOUNCEMENT

sections.question: Whether the enclosed paid political announcement by Joel H. Lubin appearing in the April 6, 1980, Southeast Daily News constitutes an election campaign status requiring filing of a campaign identification number application, given that Mr. Lubin was not a candidate in the April 8, 1980 municipal election and the expense exceeded $500.
sections.conclusion: We do not believe that the material meets that standard as interpreted by the relevant case law. Therefore, Mr. Lubin has no reporting obligation pursuant to the Political Reform Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a paid political announcement by a non-candidate, non-official published during an election period with expenses exceeding $500 trigger reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that because Mr. Lubin's communication does not "expressly advocate" an election result, no reporting obligation is triggered under the Political Reform Act despite the paid advertisement expense.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Clear advice letter with both incoming request and FPPC response. Document includes both the April 25, 1980 inquiry from City Clerk Robert L. Shand and the May 20, 1980 FPPC response from Thomas M. Sobel. The actual paid political announcement text is referenced but not fully provided in the document.

QUESTION: Whether the enclosed paid political announcement by Joel H. Lubin appearing in the April 6, 1980, Southeast Daily News constitutes an election campaign status requiring filing of a campaign identification number application, given that Mr. Lubin was not a candidate in the April 8, 1980 municipal election and the expense exceeded $500.

CONCLUSION: We do not believe that the material meets that standard as interpreted by the relevant case law. Therefore, Mr. Lubin has no reporting obligation pursuant to the Political Reform Act.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that a paid political announcement by non-candidate Joel H. Lubin does not trigger reporting obligations because it does not meet the "express advocacy" standard required under the Political Reform Act, even though the expense exceeded $500.
